# Phase 19 Design Review — Rivers / Aquatic Restoration (DCI + Barrier Optimization)

## Verdict

The plan is fundamentally sound and **close to implementation-ready**, but it must not enter Phase A until two issues are fixed. The architecture (standalone `rivers/` subpackage, dataclass-wrapping-DataFrame convention, backend-factory extraction as a sequenced Phase C0, barrier optimizers deliberately outside the Solver registry) is correctly grounded and the science (DCIp/DCId formulas, p_eff removal rule, AND-linearization, citations) is verified against Cote 2009, Neeson 2015, King & O'Hanley 2014, and the OA Shi 2018 formalization. The blockers are both correctness-of-the-headline-feature problems: (1) the closed-form LCA connectivity formula divides 0/0 → NaN whenever a barrier below the confluence is impassable (the binary MIP's own stated assumption), and (2) the single fixture meant to catch that bug has no hand-computed expected value, so it cannot. Fix those two, resolve the networkx/cost-units/objective-modes scope questions (all MEDIUM, all cheap to settle in-doc), and the plan is ready.

---

## CRITICAL
None.

## HIGH

### H1 — Closed-form LCA connectivity divides 0/0 → NaN when a downstream barrier is impassable (p=0)
*(algorithms)*

**Evidence.** The headline DCIp optimization `c_ij = root_prod(i)·root_prod(j) / root_prod(lca)²` (plan lines 128, 130, 136) yields 0/0=NaN whenever a barrier strictly downstream of `lca(i,j)` has effective passability 0. p=0 is a realistic, stated input: the diadromous binary MIP assumes "barrier present ⇒ impassable (p=0)" (line 242); any fully-blocking dam has p=0; potamodromous SA scoring hits it for any un-removed fully-impassable barrier. The cancellation that makes the ratio exact requires `root_prod(lca) ≠ 0`; when it is 0 the factor vanishes in numerator and denominator and the true value (product of barriers strictly between i and j, which can be nonzero) is destroyed. Worked: Y-tree with mouth barrier B1=0, headwaters B2=B3=0.5 ⇒ root_prod(S1)=root_prod(S2)=root_prod(S3)=0 ⇒ formula gives NaN, true c₂₃ = p(B2)·p(B3) = 0.25. Every §5 fixture uses p=0.5 only, so nothing currently gates this.

**Recommendation.** Do not use the division-based closed form when zero-passability barriers can occur. Prefer computing `c_ij` directly as the product of barriers on `path(i→lca→j)` via the already-specified `path_barriers` helper (line 130); alternatives are log-space with explicit −inf handling, or factoring out only the strictly-between barriers without forming `root_prod(lca)²`. Add a Y-tree fixture variant with a p=0 mouth barrier to the REQUIRED confluence test (extend lines 186–190) to gate the 0/0 case.

### H2 — The Y-tree confluence fixture has no hand-computed expected value, so it cannot catch H1
*(testing)*

**Evidence.** The plan correctly notes the linear chain never exercises a non-trivial LCA and calls the Y-tree "the single most important fixture to add" (line 190) — but unlike the linear chain (which pins DCId=58.333, DCIp=61.111, lines 177–178), the Y-tree is left as "Hand-compute DCIp for this network" with **no number given**. An author can satisfy this by snapshotting whatever the (possibly buggy) implementation returns. The repo convention is to commit the literal expected value (e.g. `tests/pymarxan/connectivity/test_circuit.py:22,47` assert `approx(1.0)`/`approx(0.75)`).

**Recommendation.** Compute and write the exact DCIp into the plan now, with the per-pair `c_ij` breakdown (as the linear chain has), so the test asserts an independently-derived constant. The synthesiser note in the science findings gives the key relation for the symmetric Y-tree: for the (S2,S3) pair, `c₂₃ = root_prod(S2)·root_prod(S3)/root_prod(S1)² = p2·p3`. Additionally assert that the implementation's internal `LCA(S2,S3)` resolves to the interior segment S1 (not an endpoint), directly targeting the H1 bug class.

> H1 and H2 are the same defect viewed from two angles (a numerically fragile formula + the missing fixture that would expose it). Fix them together: pin the Y-tree numbers including a p=0 variant, and switch to a path-product computation.

---

## MEDIUM

### M1 — networkx core-vs-optional decision left unresolved (architectural fork)
*(architecture)*

**Evidence.** `pyproject.toml` lists `networkx>=3.0` only under the `shiny` optional extra (line 36); core deps (21–27) omit it. The plan flags the dep must be lazy-imported "or networkx must be promoted to a core dependency — decide before Phase A" (lines 56–60), then §4 floats a pure-NumPy/dict implementation as merely "worth considering" (lines 118–121) — three live options.

**Recommendation.** Resolve before Phase A. The rooted-tree topology (downstream-pointer walk + LCA) needs no general graph library, so take the pure-NumPy/dict path: it keeps `rivers/` usable in a core-only install, avoids enlarging the base footprint, and skips lazy-import boilerplate. Make "no networkx dependency" an explicit Phase A acceptance criterion, not a "worth considering."

### M2 — `BarrierProblem.objective` declares three modes; only budget-constrained max-DCI is implemented
*(api_scope)*

**Evidence.** The dataclass exposes `objective: str = "max_dci"` with `"max_dci" | "max_gain" | "min_cost_for_target"` plus a `target_dci` field (lines 199–202), but §7 describes only budget-constrained DCI maximization for all three engines (greedy line 230, MIP line 251). No optimizer consumes `min_cost_for_target` (a different constraint shape — budget becomes objective, target becomes constraint) and `max_gain` is just `argmax(dci_after − dci_before)`, identical to `max_dci`.

**Recommendation.** For this release, ship `objective` with only the modes actually wired (`max_dci`, plus `max_gain` only if the engines genuinely branch on it). Defer `min_cost_for_target`/`target_dci` to a later phase, or raise `NotImplementedError` for unsupported `(objective × optimizer)` cells and document which are supported.

### M3 — Property tests depend on `hypothesis`, which is not (and is not planned to be) a dependency
*(testing)*

**Evidence.** Two items are specified as hypothesis property tests — "all heuristics ≤ MIP optimum … property test via hypothesis" (line 299) and the brute-force exactness sweep. `hypothesis` is absent from `pyproject.toml` dev deps (39–47) and the entire test tree (`grep -rln hypothesis tests/ src/` → nothing). A bare `import hypothesis` hard-ERRORs CI collection (`pytest … --cov-fail-under=75`, ci.yml line 48); an `importorskip` guard makes the strongest correctness checks in the plan silently skip and never gate a merge.

**Recommendation.** Either add `hypothesis>=6` to the dev extra and assert it runs in CI, or — matching the repo's seeded-loop precedent (no hypothesis anywhere today) — write `MIP == brute-force` and `heuristic ≤ MIP` as deterministic loops over a fixed seeded set of small random nets. Make the exactness check a hard, always-run test, not skippable.

---

## LOW

- **L1 — Stale version target.** Plan targets "v0.5" (lines 5, 160, 366) but `pyproject.toml:7` is already `0.6.0`. Retarget to 0.6.x/0.7.0; the YAGNI cuts (symmetric-only DCI, binary-only actions) are justified relative to a milestone, so pin them to the real one. *(grounding / api_scope — same issue, two dimensions)*

- **L2 — DCId direction default uses three conflated tokens.** Signature defaults `direction="symmetric"` (line 142) but prose says single-pass is the default (153–154) and round_trip is the alternate (∏p_b²); the literal doesn't match the documented default, and single-pass vs round-trip differ by a square — exactly the "most likely validation mismatch" (line 155). Settle on one token (e.g. `direction="single_pass"`), make the literal match the docs, and assert the default reproduces the R `dci` value. *(algorithms)*

- **L3 — `direction="round_trip"` is an unvalidated path behind a default-looking flag.** Plan ships symmetric-only and defers round-trip math for lack of a validation target (160–164), yet still exposes the keyword. Either drop the parameter from the signature now (adding a keyword-only arg later is backward-compatible) or have `round_trip` raise `NotImplementedError` until validated. *(api_scope)*

- **L4 — `segment_connectivity(form="potamodromous")` return shape undefined.** Returns `dict[int,float]` (line 144), well-defined for diadromous (per-segment cᵢ) but unspecified for the pairwise potamodromous index. Define the per-segment marginal `m_i = Σ_j w_j c_ij` (so `Σ_i w_i m_i = DCIp/100`) and test that marginals re-aggregate to scalar DCIp. *(algorithms)*

- **L5 — Binary MIP objective vs native baseline non-comparable on partial-passability nets.** MIP uses `c_i=∏y_b` (every un-removed barrier treated as p=0, lines 242–244), but `dci_before` is pinned to native partial passabilities (line 219) with the demand that "gain is comparable across greedy/SA/MIP" (222–224). On any partial-passability network the two DCI models disagree and reported MIP gain is wrong. Require `optimize_barriers_mip` to validate all non-locked-in barriers are binary (p∈{0,1}) or compute its before/after with the same binary model; add a test. *(algorithms)*

- **L6 — Cost/budget dtype unpinned in the dataclass surface.** `removal_cost` and `budget: float | None` are public but neither §4 nor §6 states integer-vs-float; §8 itself flags cross-checks "must agree on cost semantics" (line 304). Decide in §4/§6: support non-negative floats, state the optional tree-knapsack DP cross-check requires quantized costs, and document the contract on the docstrings. *(api_scope)*

- **L7 — Brute-force exactness cross-check doesn't mandate reusing production DCI/cost code.** "MIP == brute-force on every ≤8-barrier net" (line 298) doesn't say the enumerator must call the production `dci_diadromous`/`segment_connectivity` and cost accounting; an independent re-derivation can inherit the same off-by-one and both sides agree (the repo's documented heuristic-below-optimum failure mode). Specify that brute-force scores candidates via the production DCI functions (only the search is independent), and add a strict-budget boundary test (cost == budget feasible). *(testing)*

- **L8 — R `dci` validation never runs in CI and has no offline fallback.** `test_dci_validation.py` double-gates on `importorskip("rpy2")` + R-package presence (305–308); no CI step installs R, no rpy2 precedent in the repo. Add a non-skippable, R-free convention test that hand-computes BOTH the single-pass DCId (58.333 on the chain) and the round-trip value for the same p=0.5 chain and asserts which is the default; optionally commit captured R outputs as a static fixture. *(testing)*

---

## Refuted / non-issues (checked and dismissed)

- **Barrier optimizers outside the Solver/SolverRegistry universe** — Confirmed accurate but it's a *correct, explicitly-documented* design choice, not a defect (plan lines 66–71, 328). The rivers panel is never claimed to reuse the registry-driven run-panel picker. No action.
- **`STATUS_LOCKED_OUT` cited as a block range (`problem.py:16-19`) rather than the exact line 19** — The constant exists exactly as named inside the cited block; citing the whole STATUS_* block is normal practice. The reviewer prescribed no action. Non-issue.
- **Citations real/on-topic/not-retracted** — Independently re-verified all seven (Cote 2009, O'Hanley 2011, Kuby 2005, King & O'Hanley 2014, Neeson 2015, Baldan/riverconn 2022, CRAN `dci`) via scite: all real, on-topic, none retracted. Only an optional .bib year-tidy (Cote online-2008 vs print-2009). No defect.
- **Diadromous SA delta divide-by-zero via ratio update** — The plan specifies the *correct* full-subtree recompute (lines 168–169); the divide-by-zero only arises from a multiplicative ratio shortcut the plan never proposes, and the partial-passability engine never flips from p_old=0 anyway. At most a belt-and-suspenders test addition (add a p=0↔1 flip to the M3 delta test), well below MEDIUM.
- **networkx-optional makes the rivers test suite silently skip on main** — False premise: the sole CI job installs `-e ".[all]"` (ci.yml line 44), and `all` transitively pulls in `[shiny]` → networkx, so rivers tests always execute on CI regardless of the gating choice. The real residual (which gating choice to use) is captured under M1. No coverage risk.

---

## Bottom line

**Not ready to start Phase A as written — but two targeted edits unblock it.** Replace the division-based LCA formula with a path-product (or −inf-safe log-space) computation **and** pin the Y-tree fixture's exact expected values including a p=0 variant (H1+H2). Then close the three MEDIUM scope questions in-document — drop networkx (M1), trim `objective` to wired modes (M2), and de-`hypothesis` the exactness tests into seeded deterministic loops (M3). The LOW items are doc/labeling and test-hardening polish that can ride along in the same revision. None of the science or architecture needs rework.
